---
title: GPU programming concepts
author: Marie-Hélène Burle
---

:::{.def}

This section covers a few basic concepts of GPU programming so that you get a better idea of what is going on behind the scene when you use high-level GPU frameworks.

:::

## CPU vs GPU architecture

![from the [CUDA programming guide](https://docs.nvidia.com/cuda/cuda-programming-guide/index.html)](img/gpu_cpu_diagram.png){width="70%"}

**SM**: *Streaming multiprocessors* \
Collections of *thread blocks* (each *thread block* is itself a collection of *threads*).

**GPC**: *Graphics Processing Clusters* \
Collections of SMs.

**[DRAM (dynamic random access memory or dynamic RAM)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dynamic_random-access_memory) of a GPU**: *Global memory*

:::{.note}

It is called *global memory* because it is accessible to all SMs in that GPU, but it is only "global" to that GPU.

:::

### Caches

Caches on chips are hierarchical:

- **L1 caches** are the fastest. \
In CPUs, they are located inside each CPU core (so there are as many L1 caches as there are CPU cores). \
In GPUs, they are shared among all threads of an SM (so there are as many L1 caches as there are SMs).

- **L2 caches** are a little slower. \
In CPUs, they are located near each core and serve as a buffer between L1 and L2 (so there are as many L2 caches as there are CPU cores). \
In GPUs, all SMs share the same L2 (so there is a single L2, shared  among all threads on that GPU).

- The **L3 cache** is even slower (but still much faster than the DRAM). \
In CPUs, it is shared among all cores (so there is a single L3 per CPU). \
GPUs do not have an L3 cache.

## Host/device

The [CUDA programming guide](https://docs.nvidia.com/cuda/cuda-programming-guide/index.html) is a great place to learn the principles of CUDA programming. Alternative kernel-based frameworks (e.g. HIP, OpenCL), have a similar functioning.

### Terminology

| Terminology                  | Explanation              |
|------------------------------|--------------------------|
| Host                         | CPU                      |
| Host memory or system memory | CPU memory               |
| Current device               | GPU                      |
| Device memory                | GPU memory               |
| Device code                  | Code running on GPU      |
| Kernel                       | Function executed on GPU |

### Programming model

CUDA follows a heterogeneous programming model in which it manages both CPU and GPU memory.

Here is how execution happens:

- The code starts on the host.
- CUDA APIs copy data from the host memory to the current device memory.
- Kernels (GPU functions) are executed in parallel on the device: many GPU threads run computations in an [SIMT (Single Instruction, Multiple Threads)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Single_instruction,_multiple_threads) fashion.
- Finally, CUDA APIs copy the data back to the host memory.

### Multiple GPUs

When you have multiple GPUs on a computer or compute node, the NVIDIA driver queries the motherboard and orders the GPUs based on their [PCIe](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/PCI_Express) Bus ID (their physical slot on the motherboard). The GPU in the lowest PCIe slot becomes physical GPU 0, the next becomes 1, and so on.

By default, the current device is GPU 0, but you can set the current device to another GPU programmatically.
